claim: 95% of lost dogs who wander beyond their safe zones travel at most 1.8 miles (approximately 2 km) from home, per Fi GPS-collar data.
stance_target: quantitative search radius baseline — most lost dogs within 2 km
scope_conditions: Dogs fitted with Fi GPS collars who triggered safe-zone alerts, USA, large sample
quoted_support: 95% of dogs who wander beyond their designated safe zones travel at most 1.8 miles (≈ 2 km) away from home—a distance many could cover on foot in about 30 minutes.

claim: The average recovery distance for lost dogs is 0.13 miles (700 feet / approximately 2 city blocks) from home.
stance_target: median displacement of lost dogs as extremely short
quoted_support: On average, lost dogs are recovered just 0.13 miles from home. That's about 700 feet, or a little over two city blocks.

claim: 60% of lost dogs reunited with owners after shelter intake were picked up less than 1 mile from home; 42% were spotted just one block away.
stance_target: shelter data confirming extremely local displacement of lost dogs
scope_conditions: HASS project shelter data 2017-2021; Kremer 2019 study for the 42% figure
quoted_support: 60% of lost dogs that were reunited with their owner after entering a shelter were picked up less than 1 mile from home... The majority of stray dogs arriving at a shelter were found less than a mile from home, with 42% spotted just a single block away (Kremer, 2019).

claim: Finders who live outside the pet's neighborhood often transport lost dogs to shelters without checking the immediate area, bypassing the most likely reunion window.
stance_target: vehicle transport by well-meaning finders as primary displacement mechanism
quoted_support: When someone living outside the pet's neighborhood picks them up—along their work commute, for example—most take the dog directly to a shelter without checking the surrounding area first, bypassing the most likely route home.
